one_line_takeaway: An engineering proof-of-concept that a LangGraph multi-agent orchestration (Supervisor/Pentester/Recorder/Evaluator) can autonomously root a single Metasploitable 2 VM in ~150k tokens -- promising as a system design, but evaluated only qualitatively on one heavily-documented target with no baselines or benchmark.
key_finding: In a single preliminary run, BreachSeek autonomously achieved root access on the Metasploitable 2 machine using approximately 150,000 tokens, which the authors present as a proof-of-concept that the multi-agent design can carry out a multi-step exploit chain while maintaining coherence across the context. The claim is qualitative capability demonstration, not a measured success rate.
core_contribution: BreachSeek is a multi-agent LLM system that automates offensive penetration testing. Its own contribution is an engineering architecture, not a new benchmark or algorithm: a graph-based orchestration built with LangChain and LangGraph in Python that splits the pentest workflow across specialized agents to mitigate LLM context-window limits via separation of concerns. The general workflow has a Supervisor (generates action plans, picks next steps), Specialized agents (domain-specific execution), and an Evaluator (assesses output quality / task completion). The pentest-specific instantiation adds a Recorder (maintains an action summary and generates the final report) and a Pentester agent that has a shell tool and a Python tool and runs commands inside a Kali Linux environment. The system runs in a Docker-based Kali Linux container hosted on RunPod, uses Anthropic Claude 3.5 Sonnet during development (with Llama 3.1 named for future fine-tuning), and ships a web UI (NextJS front-end, FastAPI back-end) plus automatic PDF report generation. Code: https://github.com/snow10100/pena/
threat_model: Offensive / dual-use, not defensive. BreachSeek automates the attacker/pentester role -- reconnaissance, exploitation, and reporting -- against networked hosts and websites under an assumed-authorized engagement (the paper frames it as a tool for cybersecurity professionals). It actively executes exploit commands against live targets rather than only producing text. Safety controls are explicitly NOT implemented: a human-in-the-loop permission gate that would require operator approval before running specific tools/commands is deferred to Future Work, so as evaluated the agent acts autonomously with no consent checkpoint. The paper does not analyze misuse risk of an autonomous exploit agent beyond noting the planned permission system.
limitations: Evaluation is a single qualitative run against one target, with no quantitative benchmark, no success-rate statistics, and no baseline comparison (PentestGPT, Mayhem, etc. are discussed only in the Literature Review, never run head-to-head). Metasploitable 2 is an intentionally vulnerable, exhaustively documented training-VM, so exploit walkthroughs for it are pervasive on the public web and very likely in Claude 3.5 Sonnet's training data -- a single success may reflect memorized exploit knowledge rather than genuine autonomous discovery, and the paper does not control for this. The ~150k token figure is reported without cost, wall-clock time, or variance. Key safety (permission gating), robustness (fine-tuning, RAG), and generalization features are all future work. Reproducibility rests on a linked repo rather than a documented protocol.

datasets_used: Metasploitable 2 -- a single deliberately-vulnerable VM hosted on the local network (not a benchmark or dataset; the only target actually exercised)
quantitative_results: Achieved root access on the Metasploitable 2 machine using approximately 150,000 tokens (single preliminary run; the only own quantitative figure reported)
